TCX3212 Predictive Analytics¶

Tutorial/Lab 8¶

Learning Objectives

  • Understand what a time series is and how to create it in Python.
  • Visualize time series data and apply smoothing techniques
  • Make simple forecasts using moving averages and naive models.
  • Evaluate forecast accuracy using regression statistics
In [3]:
# In JupyterLab, first navigate to the directory containing College.csv
import os
os.chdir('C:/Users/sowjs/NUS Dropbox/Jin Sze Sow/Jin Sze Sow’s files/Home/BIT TCX 3212 Predictive Analytics/AY2526S2/Tutorial/T7')  # Replace with your actual path
In [4]:
# Set-up Python libraries - you need to run this but you don't need to change it
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pylab as plt
import statsmodels.formula.api as sm
from statsmodels.tsa import tsatools

Part 0: Understanding Time Series¶

Before analyzing the full Amtrak dataset, let’s start with a small example to understand a time series python object.

a) A python list has no dates. We need a DatetimeIndex to make it a time series.

  • Data values = [112, 118, 132, 129, 121, 135, 148, 148, 136, 119].
  • Start date: 01-01-2022
  • Frequency: Month-End (ME) Use pd.date_range() to create dates, combine the data into a pd.Series and display the time-series object.
In [5]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# raw numerical data representing passenger counts
values = [112, 118, 132, 129, 121, 135, 148, 148, 136, 119]

# 'ME' stands for Month-End frequency (e.g., 2022-01-31, 2022-02-28)
dates = pd.date_range(start="2022-01-01", periods=10, freq='ME')

#The 'index' is what makes this a Time Series
air_passengers = pd.Series(values, index=dates, name='Passengers')

print(air_passengers)

2022-01-31    112
2022-02-28    118
2022-03-31    132
2022-04-30    129
2022-05-31    121
2022-06-30    135
2022-07-31    148
2022-08-31    148
2022-09-30    136
2022-10-31    119
Freq: ME, Name: Passengers, dtype: int64

b) a) Extract data from March 2022 to October 2022. Plot the values with dates on the x-axis.

In [8]:
import matplotlib.dates as mdates

# Note: Pandas date slicing is INCLUSIVE of both the start and end dates
mar_to_oct = air_passengers['2022-03':'2022-10']

# Print the sliced object to verify the window
#print("--- March to October Data ---")
#print(mar_to_oct)



# Plotting with the index explicitly to ensure alignment
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
ax.plot(mar_to_oct.index, mar_to_oct.values, marker='o', color='teal', linewidth=2)
ax.set_xticks(mar_to_oct.index)  # This ensures every data point gets a label directly beneath it
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%Y-%m')) # Format the labels to 2022-03
plt.xticks(rotation=0, ha='center') 

ax.set_title('Air Passengers: Mar - Oct 2022')
ax.set_ylabel('Passengers (1000s)')
ax.grid(True, axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Time Series Analysis¶

In this exercise, you will explore and analyze monthly ridership data for Amtrak (January 1991 – March 2004). Learn to create time series objects, apply smoothing techniques, visualize trends, and make simple forecasts. You will be using the Amtrak (Amtrak.csv) data set which can be downloaded from Canvas. The dataset contains monthly ridership data from January 1991 to March 2004.

b) Load the Amtrak dataset. Construct a time-series object for the Amtrak ridership data from 01-01-1991 to 01-03-2004. Plot the monthly ridership with clearly labeled axes. What time series components appear from the plot?

In [11]:
Amtrak_df = pd.read_csv('Amtrak.csv')

# Convert the Month column to datetime object
clean_dates = pd.date_range(start='1991-01-01', periods=len(Amtrak_df), freq='MS')

# Create a time series of ridership with dates as the index
ridership_ts = pd.Series(Amtrak_df['Ridership'].values,
                         index=clean_dates, 
                         name = 'Ridership')

#print(ridership_ts)
# plot the series
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
ridership_ts.plot(ax=ax) # This uses clean_dates for the x-axis

ax.set_title('Amtrak Ridership (1991 - 2004)', fontsize=14, pad=15)
ax.set_xlabel('Time (Year-Month)')
ax.set_ylabel('Ridership (in 000s)')
ax.set_ylim(1300, 2300)
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [14]:
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose

result = seasonal_decompose(ridership_ts, model='additive', period=12)
result.plot()
plt.suptitle('Time Series Decomposition - Amtrak Ridership', fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

d) Apply a 3rd-order and 12th-order centered moving averages, and plot the smoothed series overlaid on the original time series. Compare the results and explain how the window sizes affect smoothing.

In [19]:
# centered moving average with window size = 3 and 12
ma_centered3 = ridership_ts.rolling(3, center=True).mean()
ma_centered12 = ridership_ts.rolling(12, center=True).mean()
#ma_12 = ridership_ts.rolling(window=12).mean()  # trailing by default (center=False)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 7))
ax = ridership_ts.plot(ax=ax, color='black', linewidth=0.25)
ma_centered3.plot(ax=ax, linewidth=0.5)
ma_centered12.plot(ax=ax, style='--', linewidth=1)
#ma_12.plot(ax=ax, style='--', linewidth=1)
ax.set_xlabel('Time')
ax.set_ylabel('Ridership')
ax.legend(['Ridership', 'Centered Moving Average Order 3', 'Centered Moving Average Order 12', 'Trailing moving average'])
plt.show()


#A larger w means a smoother curve

By using a 12-month window, you are effectively performing what statisticians call "Seasonality Removal." When you look at the raw data, the "dynamics" (the true growth or decline) are often hidden by the fact that ridership always peaks in the summer and drops in the winter. A 12-month average forces every calculation to include exactly one summer, one winter, one spring, and one fall, which cancels out those predictable cycles.

  • Is Amtrak fundamentally growing over the years? The 12-month MA shows the "slope" of the business without the distraction of holiday travel.
  • You can see exactly when a long-term period of growth stalls and turns into a decline (recessions, policy changes, etc.), which is often hard to spot in the "zig-zag" of monthly data.
  • Beyond the yearly "seasonal" cycle, you might see 3-5 year cycles related to the economy that are only visible once the monthly noise is gon

Note: Centered moving averages are computed by averaging across data in the past and the future of a given time point. In that sense, they cannot be used for forecasting because at the time of forecasting, the future is typically unknown. Hence, for purposes of forecasting, we use trailing moving averages

e) Compute a 12th-order trailing moving average (center=False). Explain why a trailing moving average is suitable for forecasting.

In [23]:
# Compute the 12th-order trailing moving average
# window=12: look back 12 months
# center=False: ensures it is 'trailing' (only uses past data)
Amtrak_df['MA_12'] = Amtrak_df['Ridership'].rolling(window=12).mean()


with pd.option_context('display.max_rows', None, 'display.max_columns', None):
    print(Amtrak_df['MA_12'])

In [18]:
# 3. Simple Plot to visualize the 12-month trend
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
plt.plot(ridership_ts, label='Original Data', alpha=0.3)
plt.plot(ma_12, label='12-Month Trailing MA', color='red', linewidth=2)
plt.title('Amtrak Ridership: 12-Month Trailing Moving Average')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

f) Using the last value of the 12-month trailing MA, create a naive forecast for the next 6 months (i.e., assume each future month equals the last available moving average). Present a table showing the original ridership, trailing MA, and forecasted values. Overlay the 12-month trailing moving average on the plot from part c.

In [27]:
# Compute a 12-month trailing moving average to remove seasonal "noise."
Amtrak_df['MA_12'] = Amtrak_df['Ridership'].rolling(window=12).mean()

# Use naive model for 6-step-ahead forecast
# .iloc[-1] uses "Integer Location" start counting from the end [-1] is the last row in the dataset
last_ma = Amtrak_df['MA_12'].iloc[-1]  # last available MA

# Generate 6 new months of dates, starting 1 month after the last date in our data.
# We create a new DataFrame for the future. 
# 'Ridership' is None (unknown), but 'MA_12' is filled with our constant prediction (last_ma).
forecast_months = pd.date_range(start=Amtrak_df.index[-1] + pd.DateOffset(months=1), periods=6, freq='MS')
forecast_df = pd.DataFrame({'Ridership': [None]*6, 'MA_12': [last_ma]*6}, index=forecast_months)

# Combine original data and forecast
full_df = pd.concat([Amtrak_df, forecast_df])

# Print the last 15 rows including forecast
#print(full_df.tail(20))

with pd.option_context('display.max_rows', None, 'display.max_columns', None):
    print(full_df)

In [28]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# --- Step 1: Initialize the Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))

# Original ridership
plt.plot(full_df.index, full_df['Ridership'], linewidth=0.8)

# 12-month trailing moving average (includes forecast extension)
plt.plot(full_df.index, full_df['MA_12'], linewidth=2)

# Vertical line to mark forecast start
# plt.axvline draws a vertical line at the LAST date of the original dataset (Amtrak_df).
# This visually separates known historical data (left) from the naive forecast (right).
plt.axvline(Amtrak_df.index[-1], linestyle='--')

plt.title('Amtrak Ridership with 12-Month Trailing MA and Naive Forecast')
plt.xlabel('Time')
plt.ylabel('Ridership (in 000s)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

g) Partition the data by using the last 36 months as a validation set and the remaining observations as a training set. (Set nValid = 36)

In [21]:
nValid = 36  # last 36 months (3 years) for validation
nTrain = len(ridership_ts) - nValid  # the rest for training

train_ts = ridership_ts[:nTrain]  # training data
valid_ts = ridership_ts[nTrain:]  # validation data

h) The naive forecast is the simplest forecasting method in time series analysis. It assumes that the value of the series in the next time period will be equal to the last observed value in the training data. • Using the training time series, generate a naive forecast for the entire validation period. Each month in the validation set should be predicted using the last value from the training set.

In [22]:
# generate the naive forecast
naive_pred = pd.Series(train_ts.iloc[-1], index=valid_ts.index)
print(naive_pred)

i) The seasonal naive forecast accounts for seasonality by assuming that future values repeat the same seasonal pattern observed in the most recent season. • Assume the data has a 12-month seasonal cycle. Generate a seasonal naive forecast for the validation period by repeating the last 12 months of the training data until the validation period is fully covered.. j)

In [18]:

In [23]:
last_season = train_ts.iloc[-12:]
seasonal_pred = pd.Series(pd.concat([last_season]*5)[:len(valid_ts)].values,index=valid_ts.index)
print(seasonal_pred)
# Repeats the last observed seasonal cycle 5 times Jan, Feb, ....Dec,

j) Using the training and validation splits, plot the actual ridership together with the naive and seasonal naive forecasts on a single time-series chart.

In [20]:
# plot forecasts and actual in the training and validation sets
ax = train_ts.plot(color='C0', linewidth=0.75, figsize=(9,7))
valid_ts.plot(ax=ax, color='C0', linestyle='dashed', linewidth=0.75)
ax.set_xlim('1990', '2006-6')
ax.set_ylim(1300, 2600)
ax.set_xlabel('Time')
ax.set_ylabel('Ridership (in 000s)')

naive_pred.plot(ax=ax, color='green')
seasonal_pred.plot(ax=ax, color='orange')

# determine coordinates for drawing the arrows and lines
one_month = pd.Timedelta('31 days')
xtrain = (min(train_ts.index), max(train_ts.index) - one_month)
xvalid = (min(valid_ts.index) + one_month, max(valid_ts.index) - one_month)
xfuture = (max(valid_ts.index) + one_month, '2006')
xtv = xtrain[1] + 0.5 * (xvalid[0] - xtrain[1])
xvf = xvalid[1] + 0.5 * (xfuture[0] - xvalid[1])
ax.add_line(plt.Line2D(xtrain, (2450, 2450), color='black', linewidth=0.5))
ax.add_line(plt.Line2D(xvalid, (2450, 2450), color='black', linewidth=0.5))
ax.add_line(plt.Line2D(xfuture, (2450, 2450), color='black', linewidth=0.5))
ax.text('1995', 2500, 'Training')
ax.text('2001-9', 2500, 'Validation')
ax.text('2004-7', 2500, 'Future')
ax.axvline(x=xtv, ymin=0, ymax=1, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
ax.axvline(x=xvf, ymin=0, ymax=1, color='black', linewidth=0.5)
plt.show()

k) Implement a Python function regression_stats(actual, forecast) to evaluate forecast accuracy. The function should accept observed and forecasted time-series values as inputs and compute RMSE, MAE, MPE, and MAPE. Use this function to assess and compare different forecasting methods.

In [24]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

def regression_stats(actual, forecast):
    """
    Compute forecast accuracy statistics:
    ME, RMSE, MAE, MPE, MAPE
    """
    # convert to numpy arrays
    actual = np.array(actual)
    forecast = np.array(forecast)
    
    # errors
    errors = actual - forecast
    
    # Mean Error
    ME = np.mean(errors)
    
    # Root Mean Squared Error
    RMSE = np.sqrt(np.mean(errors**2))
    
    # Mean Absolute Error
    MAE = np.mean(np.abs(errors))
    
    # Mean Percentage Error
    MPE = np.mean(errors / actual) * 100
    
    # Mean Absolute Percentage Error
    MAPE = np.mean(np.abs(errors / actual)) * 100
    
    # print results
    print("Regression statistics")
    print(f"Mean Error (ME) : {ME:.4f}")
    print(f"Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) : {RMSE:.4f}")
    print(f"Mean Absolute Error (MAE) : {MAE:.4f}")
    print(f"Mean Percentage Error (MPE) : {MPE:.4f}")
    print(f"Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) : {MAPE:.4f}")
    print("")

# Example usage with your forecasts
regression_stats(valid_ts, naive_pred)
regression_stats(valid_ts, seasonal_pred)
# Only take the trailing MA values that align with the validation set
ma_12_valid = ma_12[-nValid:]
regression_stats(valid_ts, ma_12_valid)

In [ ]:
# Training forecasts
naive_train = train_ts.shift(1).dropna()
actual_train_naive = train_ts[1:]

seasonal_train = train_ts.shift(12).dropna()
actual_train_seasonal = train_ts[12:]

# Compute stats
print("Naive forecast on training:")
regression_stats(actual_train_naive, naive_train)

print("Seasonal naive forecast on training:")
regression_stats(actual_train_seasonal, seasonal_train)
In [ ]:
